attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman is being assessed in a psychiatry outpatient clinic for a history of low mood for the past several years as well as anxiety. She migrated from Iraq six years ago with her family and previously worked as a lawyer, but has been unable to secure work in Australia. On specific questioning, she is very anxious a lot of the time about dying and that she has a serious underlying medical condition. These fears are worst when she is under stress and she gets palpitations, feels sweaty and dizzy. She is preoccupied by her blood pressure and feels if it goes up she will have a heart attack or stroke. She has previously been told her blood pressure was high and one of her grandparents died from a heart attack many years ago. She has stopped leaving the house or exercising now for fear increasing her blood pressure and causing a heart attack, which she worries about for many hours a day. Her medications include escitalopram for the past five years. She feels this treatment has had no great effect but she frequently avoids doctors. She believes her symptoms have worsened since her daughters have started school and she worries she will die before they grow up. A brief physical examination reveals a normal blood pressure and no other signs of concern.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The history describes predominantly anxiety symptoms over the last six years that have worsened recently and are associated with low mood. She is particularly worried about dying from a heart attack and believes an elevated blood pressure will kill her secondary to such an attack. She has a preoccupation with this despite having no chest pain or SOB to suggest any ischaemic heart disease and a lack of risk factors other than minor family history. She spends a large portion of her day thinking about this and it has prevented her from working or leaving the house. This picture best fits with an illness anxiety disorder. 

Closely related is somatic symptom disorder however patients would usually focus more on the symptoms they are getting and be concerned about what they might indicate. This patient is not particularly concerned by her symptoms but rather is primarily worried about having a heart attack from them. Further, the symptoms only occur at times of stress and the anxiety is there much more of the time. 

Generalised anxiety disorder is unlikely as she has a specific preoccupation and does not describe anxiety in multiple situations previously.

She describes some features of panic attacks, however, she is not fearful or preoccupied about the attacks themselves and panic disorder does not then fit the best to explain her presentation.

She has a fear of elevated blood pressure but this is not an object or physical situation and hence not a Specific Phobia and her avoidance of going out or exercise she explains by thoughts that elevating her blood pressure will cause a heart attack - it is the end result of a heart attack that she is avoiding.
learningPoints: In patients with excessive worry about serious diagnosis with minimal symptoms consider illness anxiety disorder.
references: Scarella TM, Boland R, Barsky AJ. Illness Anxiety Disorder: Psychopathology, Epidemiology, Clinical Characteristics, and Treatment. Psychosomatic Medicine 2019; 81:398-407
